coredata.dc:title: Lithium-ion Batteries Modeling and Optimization Strategies for Sinusoidal Alternating Current Heating at Low Temperature
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jun-qiu
Sun, Danni
coredata.dc:description: A thermo-electric coupled model of sinusoidal alternating current (AC) heating for lithium-ion power batteries is proposed in this paper. The model combines the equivalent circuit model with the heat generation model in frequency domain. The model is validated against the experimental results of sinusoidal AC heating with different amplitudes and frequencies of current. An optimal strategy of sinusoidal AC heating based on battery safe operating voltage limit is proposed . It is found that in the high frequency region, the maximum current amplitude under the safety voltage limit is larger, the maximum heat generation rate of the battery is larger, and the battery can be heated from -20°C to 0°C within 480s.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
sinusoidal AC heating
thermo-electric coupled model
optimal strategy

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: ScienceDirect Available online at www.sciencedirect.comAvailable online at w.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling. The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling Assessing the feasibility of using the heat demand-outdoor temperature function for a long-term district heat demand forecast I. AndriÄ‡a,b,c*, A. Pinaa, P. FerrÃ£oa, J. Fournierb., B. LacarriÃ¨rec, O. Le Correc aIN+ Center for Innovation, Technology and Policy Research - Instituto Superior TÃ©cnico, Av. Rovisco Pais 1, 1049-001 Lisbon, Portugal bVeolia Recherche & Innovation, 291 Avenue Dreyfous Daniel, 78520 Limay, France cDÃ©partement SystÃ¨mes Ã‰nergÃ©tiques et Environnement - IMT Atlantique, 4 rue Alfred Kastler, 44300 Nantes, France Abstract District heating networks are commonly addressed in the literature as one of the most effective solutions for decreasing the greenhouse gas emissions from the building sector. These systems require high investments which are returned through the heat sales. Due to the changed climate conditions and building renovation policies, heat demand in the future could decrease, prolonging the investment return period. The main scope of this paper is to assess the feasibility of using the heat demand â€“ outdoor temperature function for heat demand forecast. The district of Alvalade, located in Lisbon (Portugal), was used as a case study. The district is consisted of 665 buildings that vary in both construction period and typology. Three weather scenarios (low, medium, high) and three district renovation scenarios were developed (shallow, intermediate, deep). To estimate the error, obtained heat demand values were compared with results from a dynamic heat demand model, previously developed and validated by the authors. The results showed that when only weather change is considered, the margin of error could be acceptable for some applications (the error in annual demand was lower than 20% for all weather scenarios considered). However, after introducing renovation scenarios, the error value increased up to 59.5% (depending on the weather and renovation scenarios combination considered). The value of slope coefficient increased on average within the range of 3.8% up to 8% per decade, that corresponds to the decrease in the number of heating hours of 22-139h during the heating season (depending on the combination of weather and renovation scenarios considered). On the other hand, function intercept increased for 7.8-12.7% per decade (depending on the coupled scenarios). The values suggested could be used to modify the function parameters for the scenarios considered, and improve the accuracy of heat demand estimations. Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling. Keywords: Heat demand; Forecast; Climate change Energy Procedia 152 ( 8) 562 567 1876-6102 Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the CUE2018-Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems. 10.1016/j.egypro.2018.09.211 10.1016/j.egypro.2018.09.211 Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific c mmittee f the CUE2018-Applied Energy Symposium and Fo um 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems. 1876-6102 CUE2018-Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, 5â€“7 June 2018, Shanghai, China Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018. Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018, 5â€“7 June 2018, Shang i, China Lithium-ion Batteries Modeling and Optimization Strate ies for Sinusoidal Alternating Current Heating at Low Temperature Jun-qiu Lia,*, Danni Suna aCollaborative Innovation Center of Electric Vehicles in Beijing, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing, 100081,China. Abstract A thermo-electric coupled model of sinusoidal alternating current (AC) heating for lithium-ion power batteries is proposed in this paper. The model combines the equivalent circuit model with the heat generation model in frequency domain. The model is validated against the experimental results of sinusoidal AC heating with different amplitudes and frequencies of current. An optimal strategy of sinusoidal AC heating based on battery safe operating voltage limit is proposed . It is found that in the high frequency region, the maximum current amplitude under the safety voltage limit is larger, the maximum heat generation rate of the battery is larger, and the battery can be heated from -20Â°C to 0Â°C within 480s. Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018. Keywords: Lithium-ion battery, sinusoidal AC heating, thermo-electric coupled model, optimal strategy 1. Introduction Recently, lithium-ion batteries have become the most promising and competitive vehicle power batteries with advantages such as high specific power, voltage, large capacity, long cycle life and little pollution. However, lithium-ion power batteries have problems such as difficulty in charging, deterioration of discharge performance, and decrease in dischargeable capacity at low temperatures, which severely shorten the driving range of electric vehicles and limit their application in cold regions. Therefore, it is necessary to heat the battery before charging at a low temperature to improve its performance. Lots of previous researches focus on external heating methods, that is, the heat is generated by the external heat- * Corresponding author. Tel.:086-010-68940589; fax: 086-010-68914842. E-mail address: lijunqiu@bit.edu.cn Available online at www.sciencedirect.com Scie ceDire t Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Co yright Â© 2018 Els vier Ltd. All rig ts reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018. Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low c rbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018, 5â€“7 June 2018, Shanghai, China Lithium-ion Batteries Modeling and Optimization Strategies for Sinusoidal Alternating Current Heating at Low Temperature Jun-qiu Lia,*, Danni Suna aCollaborative Innovation Center of Electric Vehicles in Beijing, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing, 100081,China. Abstract A thermo-electric coupled model of s nusoidal alternating curren (AC) heati g for lithium-io power batteries is proposed in th p per. The model combines the equiva ent circuit model with the heat generation model in frequency domain. The model is val dated against the experimental results of sinusoidal AC h ating with differ nt amplitudes and frequencies of current. An optimal strategy of sinusoidal AC heating based on battery safe operatin voltage limit is proposed . It is found that in the high fr quency region, the maximum curre t ampli u e under the safety vol age limit is larger, the maximum heat generation rate of the battery is larger, and the battery can be heated from -20Â°C to 0Â°C within 480s. Copyr ght Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018. Keywords: Lithium-ion battery, sinusoidal AC heating, thermo-electric coupled model, optimal strategy 1. Introduction Recently, lit ium- on batteries hav become the most promising and ompetitive vehicle power batteries with advantages such as high specific ower, voltage, large capacity, lon cycle life and little pollution. However, lithium-ion power batteries have problems such as difficulty in charging, deterioration of discharge per ormance, and decrease in discha geable capacity at low temperatures, which s v rely sh rt n the driving range of electric vehicles and limi their application in cold regions. Therefore, it is necessary to heat the battery before charging at a low temperature to improve its performanc . Lots of previous researches focus on external heating methods, that is, the heat is generated by the external heat- * Corresponding author. Tel.:086-010-68940589; fax: 086-010-68914842. E-mail address: lijunqiu@bit.edu.cn 2 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 generating device. It takes a long time, losses a large part of heat, and causes uneven heating. Therefore, the advantages of internal heating are highlighted by stimulating the battery to generate heat itself. Ruan, H. et al. [1] considered the influence of the sinusoidal polarization voltage and proposed that the optimal frequency for sinusoidal AC heating is 1377 Hz. The battery can heat up from -15.4Â°C to 5.6Â°C within 338s. Jiang, J. et al. [2] proposed frequency-dependent equation to describe polarization parameters, simplifying the model. Zhang, J. et al. [3] found that the lower frequency and the higher amplitude of the sinusoidal AC, the faster the temperature rise of the battery. Ge, H. et al. [4] established a three-electrode model for a lithium-ion battery and proposed a method for internal preheating by sinusoidal AC with lithium-ion deposition prevention. Zhu, J. et al. [5] found that the maximum temperature rise is obtained at a frequency of 30 Hz when sinusoidal and square waves are used to heat the battery for AC heating, and the heating effect of the square wave is better. These researches have not yet obtained the optimal sinusoidal AC heating strategy. In this paper, a thermo-electric coupled model of sinusoidal AC heating for lithium-ion power battery at low temperature is established, and the parameters of the model are identified by genetic algorithm. Then, sinusoidal AC heating experiments are conducted under different conditions to validate of the model. Finally, an optimal strategy for sinusoidal AC heating is proposed, which can heat the battery from -20Â°C to 0Â°C within 480s, and the temperature distribution is even. 2. Model development 2.1 Equivalent Circuit Model Based on the electrochemical reaction mechanism of the battery, an equivalent circuit model in frequency domain is established as shown in Fig. 1. 0R represents ohmic resistance, ctR represents polarization resistance, SEIR is solid electrolyte interphase resistance (SEI), dlC is electric double-layer capacitance, SEIC is the capacitance of SEI and L is Inductance. The Warburg diffusion impedance is neglected because it is very small in the frequency range (>0.1 Hz) used in this study. During the AC heating process, the heat generation rate is related to the real part of the impedance. L Ro Rct Cdl RSEI CSEI UOC Fig. 1. Equivalent circuit model The real part of the impedance can be expressed as ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© 20 2 2 2 2 2 , 1 (2 ) 1 (2 ) ct SEI Re ct dl SEI SEI R T R T Z T f R T f R T C f R T Cï�° ï�° ï€½ ï€« ï€« ï€« ï€« (1) The total impedance can be expressed as ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© 2 2 2 2 0 2 2 2 2 2 22 2 2 2 2 2 , 2 1 (2 ) 1 (2 )1 (2 ) 1 (2 ) ct SEI ct dl SEI SEI ct dl ct dlSEI SEI SEI SEI R T R T f R T C f R T C Z T f R T j fL f R T C f R T Cf R T C f R T C ï�° ï�° ï�° ï�° ï�°ï�° ï�° ïƒ© ïƒ¹ ïƒ© ïƒ¹ïƒ— ïƒ— ï€½ ï€« ï€« ï€« ï€­ ï€­ïƒª ïƒº ïƒª ïƒº ï€« ï€«ï€« ï€«ïƒª ïƒº ïƒª ïƒºïƒ« ïƒ» ïƒ« ïƒ» (2) where f is the current frequency. 2.2. Energy conservation model For 18650 cells, the internal and surface temperatures are quite close even under a large current excitation [6]. The energy conservation equation inside the battery can be expressed as: t Tm C q q t ï‚¶ ïƒ— ïƒ— ï€½ ï€­ ï‚¶ (3) where m is the mass of the battery, C is the specific heat capacity, t is the time, T is the battery temperature, tq is the heat flux transferred from the battery to the environment and q is the heat generation rate of the battery. The convective heat dissipation of the surface can be expressed by the Newtonâ€™s law of cooling. (T T )t aq h Sï€½ ïƒ— ïƒ— ï€­ (4) where h is the equivalent heat transfer coefficient, S is the battery surface area and aT is the ambient temperature. The reversible heat generation can be ignored for its algebraic sum equal to zero during one cycle of AC heating. When the current applied describes as this: Jun-qiu Li et al. / Energy Procedia 152 (2018) 562â€“567 563 Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018. Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018, 5â€“7 June 2018, Shanghai, China Lithium-ion Batteries Modeling and Optimization Strategies for Sinusoidal Alternating Current Heating at Low Temperature Jun-qiu Lia,*, Danni Suna aCollaborative Innovation Center of Electric Vehicles in Beijing, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing, 100081,China. Abstract A thermo-electric coupled model of sinusoidal alternating current (AC) heating for lithium-ion power batteries is proposed in this paper. The model combines the equivalent circuit model with the heat generation model in frequency domain. The model is validated against the experimental results of sinusoidal AC heating with different amplitudes and frequencies of current. An optimal strategy of sinusoidal AC heating based on battery safe operating voltage limit is proposed . It is found that in the high frequency region, the maximum current amplitude under the safety voltage limit is larger, the maximum heat generation rate of the battery is larger, and the battery can be heated from -20Â°C to 0Â°C within 480s. Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018. Keywords: Lithium-ion battery, sinusoidal AC heating, thermo-electric coupled model, optimal strategy 1. Introduction Recently, lithium-ion batteries have become the most promising and competitive vehicle power batteries with advantages such as high specific power, voltage, large capacity, long cycle life and little pollution. However, lithium-ion power batteries have problems such as difficulty in charging, deterioration of discharge performance, and decrease in dischargeable capacity at low temperatures, which severely shorten the driving range of electric vehicles and limit their application in cold regions. Therefore, it is necessary to heat the battery before charging at a low temperature to improve its performance. Lots of previous researches focus on external heating methods, that is, the heat is generated by the external heat- * Corresponding author. Tel.:086-010-68940589; fax: 086-010-68914842. E-mail address: lijunqiu@bit.edu.cn Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Co yright Â© 2018 Els vier Ltd. All rig ts reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018. Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low c rbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018, 5â€“7 June 2018, Shanghai, China Lithium-ion Batteries Modeling and Optimization Strategies for Sinusoidal Alternating Current Heating at Low Temperature Jun-qiu Lia,*, Danni Suna aCollaborative Innovation Center of Electric Vehicles in Beijing, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing, 100081,China. Abstract A thermo-electric coupled model of s nusoidal alternating curren (AC) heati g for lithium-io power batteries is proposed in th p per. The model combines the equiva ent circuit model with the heat generation model in frequency domain. The model is val dated against the experimental results of sinusoidal AC h ating with differ nt amplitudes and frequencies of current. An optimal strategy of sinusoidal AC heating based on battery safe operatin voltage limit is proposed . It is found that in the high fr quency region, the maximum curre t ampli u e under the safety vol age limit is larger, the maximum heat generation rate of the battery is larger, and the battery can be heated from -20Â°C to 0Â°C within 480s. Copyr ght Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018. Keywords: Lithium-ion battery, sinusoidal AC heating, thermo-electric coupled model, optimal strategy 1. Introduction Recently, lit ium- on batteries hav become the most promising and ompetitive vehicle power batteries with advantages such as high specific ower, voltage, large capacity, lon cycle life and little pollution. However, lithium-ion power batteries have problems such as difficulty in charging, deterioration of discharge per ormance, and decrease in discha geable capacity at low temperatures, which s v rely sh rt n the driving range of electric vehicles and limi their application in cold regions. Therefore, it is necessary to heat the battery before charging at a low temperature to improve its performanc . Lots of previous researches focus on external heating methods, that is, the heat is generated by the external heat- * Corresponding author. Tel.:086-010-68940589; fax: 086-010-68914842. E-mail address: lijunqiu@bit.edu.cn 2 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 generating device. It takes a long time, losses a large part of heat, and causes uneven heating. Therefore, the advantages of internal heating are highlighted by stimulating the battery to generate heat itself. Ruan, H. et al. [1] considered the influence of the sinusoidal polarization voltage and proposed that the optimal frequency for sinusoidal AC heating is 1377 Hz. The battery can heat up from -15.4Â°C to 5.6Â°C within 338s. Jiang, J. et al. [2] proposed frequency-dependent equation to describe polarization parameters, simplifying the model. Zhang, J. et al. [3] found that the lower frequency and the higher amplitude of the sinusoidal AC, the faster the temperature rise of the battery. Ge, H. et al. [4] established a three-electrode model for a lithium-ion battery and proposed a method for internal preheating by sinusoidal AC with lithium-ion deposition prevention. Zhu, J. et al. [5] found that the maximum temperature rise is obtained at a frequency of 30 Hz when sinusoidal and square waves are used to heat the battery for AC heating, and the heating effect of the square wave is better. These researches have not yet obtained the optimal sinusoidal AC heating strategy. In this paper, a thermo-electric coupled model of sinusoidal AC heating for lithium-ion power battery at low temperature is established, and the parameters of the model are identified by genetic algorithm. Then, sinusoidal AC heating experiments are conducted under different conditions to validate of the model. Finally, an optimal strategy for sinusoidal AC heating is proposed, which can heat the battery from -20Â°C to 0Â°C within 480s, and the temperature distribution is even. 2. Model development 2.1 Equivalent Circuit Model Based on the electrochemical reaction mechanism of the battery, an equivalent circuit model in frequency domain is established as shown in Fig. 1. 0R represents ohmic resistance, ctR represents polarization resistance, SEIR is solid electrolyte interphase resistance (SEI), dlC is electric double-layer capacitance, SEIC is the capacitance of SEI and L is Inductance. The Warburg diffusion impedance is neglected because it is very small in the frequency range (>0.1 Hz) used in this study. During the AC heating process, the heat generation rate is related to the real part of the impedance. L Ro Rct Cdl RSEI CSEI UOC Fig. 1. Equivalent circuit model The real part of the impedance can be expressed as ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© 20 2 2 2 2 2 , 1 (2 ) 1 (2 ) ct SEI Re ct dl SEI SEI R T R T Z T f R T f R T C f R T Cï�° ï�° ï€½ ï€« ï€« ï€« ï€« (1) The total impedance can be expressed as ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© ï€¨ ï€© 2 2 2 2 0 2 2 2 2 2 22 2 2 2 2 2 , 2 1 (2 ) 1 (2 )1 (2 ) 1 (2 ) ct SEI ct dl SEI SEI ct dl ct dlSEI SEI SEI SEI R T R T f R T C f R T C Z T f R T j fL f R T C f R T Cf R T C f R T C ï�° ï�° ï�° ï�° ï�°ï�° ï�° ïƒ© ïƒ¹ ïƒ© ïƒ¹ïƒ— ïƒ— ï€½ ï€« ï€« ï€« ï€­ ï€­ïƒª ïƒº ïƒª ïƒº ï€« ï€«ï€« ï€«ïƒª ïƒº ïƒª ïƒºïƒ« ïƒ» ïƒ« ïƒ» (2) where f is the current frequency. 2.2. Energy conservation model For 18650 cells, the internal and surface temperatures are quite close even under a large current excitation [6]. The energy conservation equation inside the battery can be expressed as: t Tm C q q t ï‚¶ ïƒ— ïƒ— ï€½ ï€­ ï‚¶ (3) where m is the mass of the battery, C is the specific heat capacity, t is the time, T is the battery temperature, tq is the heat flux transferred from the battery to the environment and q is the heat generation rate of the battery. The convective heat dissipation of the surface can be expressed by the Newtonâ€™s law of cooling. (T T )t aq h Sï€½ ïƒ— ïƒ— ï€­ (4) where h is the equivalent heat transfer coefficient, S is the battery surface area and aT is the ambient temperature. The reversible heat generation can be ignored for its algebraic sum equal to zero during one cycle of AC heating. When the current applied describes as this: 564 Jun-qiu Li et al. / Energy Procedia 152 (2018) 562â€“567 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 3 1(t) sin( t )i A f ï�ªï€½ ï€« (5) The heat generation rate can be described as: 2 Re )2 ,(Aq TZ fïƒ¦ ïƒ¶ï€½ ïƒ—ïƒ§ ïƒ· ïƒ¨ ïƒ¸ (6) Considering the battery safety voltage limit, the upper limit current is: max min max min ,| Z | | Z | oc ocU U U UA ï€­ ï€­ïƒ¦ ïƒ¶ï€½ ïƒ§ ïƒ· ïƒ¨ ïƒ¸ (7) where the upper limit of voltage is 4.2V and the lower limit of voltage is 2.8V, | Z | represents the module of total impedance, which can be expressed as: 2 2 Re Im| Z | = Z Zï€« (8) 2.3 Extraction of model parameters There are some parameters of the model need to be determined: specific heat capacity C ; convective heat transfer coefficient h ; 0R , ctR , SEIR , dlC , SEIC and L in the equivalent circuit model. These parameters can be obtained by genetic algorithm based on experimental data. The flow of genetic algorithm is shown in Fig. 2. For the sinusoidal AC heating model, the genetic algorithm fitness function is determined based on the least square principle of the calculated temperature and measured temperature. 2 1 min{ [ ( ) ( )] } n m i J T i T i ï€½ ï€½ ï€­ïƒ¥ (9) Generate first-generation populations randomly Into fitness function for calculation Eliminate part of the individual does not meet Produce the next generation by mating and genetic mutation Select the most suitable individuals Whether to achieve the number of iterations Yes No Fig. 2. Flow chart of genetic algorithm calculation. 3. Experiments In this paper, a battery module consisting of three 18650 cells in parallel is used in the experiment. The 18650 cylindrical lithium-ion battery parameters are shown in Table 1. As shown in Fig. 3, the incubator can supply a low temperature condition. The Bipolar Power supplies sinusoidal AC. The electrochemical workstation is used to measure the electrochemical impedance spectrum of the battery. Battery management system (BMS) can collect the battery temperature and voltage. Digatron is used to charge and discharge the battery. Impedance can be obtained from the EIS test. The sinusoidal AC heating experimental details are shown in Table 2. Table 1. Parameters of cell and battery pack Parameter name Parameter value Rated capacity of cell 2150mAh@0.33C Charging cut-off voltage of cell 4.2Â±0.05V Discharging cut-off voltage of cell 2.75Â±0.05V Resistance of cell â‰¤35mâ„¦ Charging cut-off voltage of pack 4.2Â±0.05V Discharging cut-off voltage of pack 2.75Â±0.05V Rated capacity of module 6450mAh Incubator BMS Bipolar Power Supply Digatron Monitor EIS Fig. 3 Schematic diagram of experimental equipment Table 2. Test item of heating experiment Initial temperature Frequency Amplitude(A) Heating time -20â„ƒ 8 Hz 1C, 1.5C, 2C 20 min -15â„ƒ 8 Hz 1C, 1.5C, 2C 20 min -15â„ƒ 1, 4, 16 Hz 2C 20 min -10â„ƒ 8 Hz 1C, 1.5C, 2C 20 min -20â„ƒ Various 1C, 1.5C, 2C 15 min -20â„ƒ 8Hz Various 15 min -20â„ƒ Various Various 15 min 4 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 4. Results and discussion 4.1. Parameters of the model Based on the experimental data of the battery in the AC heating process, the parameters identified by the genetic algorithm are: (1) Battery specific heat capacity C is 992.87 J/(kgÂ·K); (2) Heat transfer coefficient h is 14.07 W/ (m2Â·K); (3) The relationship between 0R , ctR , SEIR and T in the equivalent circuit model is shown in Fig. 4. 4.2. Model verification with constant frequency and amplitude A heating experiment was conducted, where the sinusoidal AC frequency is set to 8Hz, the amplitude is set to 1.5C and 2C, the initial incubator temperature is set to -20â„ƒ. The sinusoidal AC heating model simulation and experiment results are shown in Fig. 5 and Fig. 6.The temperature rising simulation curve of the model well reflects the actual temperature change of the battery. The maximum temperature error between simulation and experimental results is 1.35Â°C, the average temperature error is 0.27Â°C, and it is within a reasonable range. It sufficiently suggests that the model is correct and effective. The deviation between simulation and experimental results is mainly due to the insulation condition in the experiment is not ideal, and the parameters such as the heat transfer coefficient in the simulation can be modified to better simulate the experimental conditions to reduce the deviation. 0 500 1000 1500 -25 -20 -15 -10 -5 0 T em p er a tu re ( â„ƒ ) Time (s) Simulation Experiment 0 200 400 600 800 1000 1200 -25 -20 -15 -10 -5 0 T em p er a tu re ( â„ƒ ) Time (s) Simulation Experiment Fig. 4. Variation of the resistance Fig. 5. Simulation and experiment results of 1.5C/8Hz Fig. 6. Simulation and experiment results of 2C/8Hz 4.3. Optimal sinusoidal AC heating strategy The sinusoidal AC heating experiments were conducted at different amplitudes with the same frequency and at different frequencies with the same amplitude. The heat generation effect is shown in Fig. 7 and Fig. 8. 0 200 400 600 800 1000 1200 1400 -25 -20 -15 -10 -5 0 T em pe ra tu re ï¼ˆ â„ƒ ï¼‰ Time (s) 1C 8Hz 1.5C 8Hz 2C 8Hz -200 0 200 400 600 800 1000 1200 1400 -15 -10 -5 0 5 T em p er a tu re ( â„ƒ ) Time (s) 2C 1Hz 2C 4Hz 2C 8Hz 2C 16Hz Fig. 7. Heat effect of different amplitudes Fig. 8. Heat effect of different frequencies Fig. 9. Evolution of the total impedance. It can be seen from Fig. 7, the heat generation effect becomes better with the increase of the current amplitude at the same frequency. However, when the current amplitude is too large, the voltage of the battery may exceed the safety limit. Over-voltage and under-voltage will cause side reactions, the electrolyte and the active material decomposed, a large amount of heat released, gas generated, and thermal runaway of the battery. It can be seen from Fig. 8, the heat generation effect becomes better with the current frequency decreases at the same amplitudes. However, during the experiment, it can be observed that when the frequency is low and the Jun-qiu Li et al. / Energy Procedia 152 (2018) 562â€“567 565 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 3 1(t) sin( t )i A f ï�ªï€½ ï€« (5) The heat generation rate can be described as: 2 Re )2 ,(Aq TZ fïƒ¦ ïƒ¶ï€½ ïƒ—ïƒ§ ïƒ· ïƒ¨ ïƒ¸ (6) Considering the battery safety voltage limit, the upper limit current is: max min max min ,| Z | | Z | oc ocU U U UA ï€­ ï€­ïƒ¦ ïƒ¶ï€½ ïƒ§ ïƒ· ïƒ¨ ïƒ¸ (7) where the upper limit of voltage is 4.2V and the lower limit of voltage is 2.8V, | Z | represents the module of total impedance, which can be expressed as: 2 2 Re Im| Z | = Z Zï€« (8) 2.3 Extraction of model parameters There are some parameters of the model need to be determined: specific heat capacity C ; convective heat transfer coefficient h ; 0R , ctR , SEIR , dlC , SEIC and L in the equivalent circuit model. These parameters can be obtained by genetic algorithm based on experimental data. The flow of genetic algorithm is shown in Fig. 2. For the sinusoidal AC heating model, the genetic algorithm fitness function is determined based on the least square principle of the calculated temperature and measured temperature. 2 1 min{ [ ( ) ( )] } n m i J T i T i ï€½ ï€½ ï€­ïƒ¥ (9) Generate first-generation populations randomly Into fitness function for calculation Eliminate part of the individual does not meet Produce the next generation by mating and genetic mutation Select the most suitable individuals Whether to achieve the number of iterations Yes No Fig. 2. Flow chart of genetic algorithm calculation. 3. Experiments In this paper, a battery module consisting of three 18650 cells in parallel is used in the experiment. The 18650 cylindrical lithium-ion battery parameters are shown in Table 1. As shown in Fig. 3, the incubator can supply a low temperature condition. The Bipolar Power supplies sinusoidal AC. The electrochemical workstation is used to measure the electrochemical impedance spectrum of the battery. Battery management system (BMS) can collect the battery temperature and voltage. Digatron is used to charge and discharge the battery. Impedance can be obtained from the EIS test. The sinusoidal AC heating experimental details are shown in Table 2. Table 1. Parameters of cell and battery pack Parameter name Parameter value Rated capacity of cell 2150mAh@0.33C Charging cut-off voltage of cell 4.2Â±0.05V Discharging cut-off voltage of cell 2.75Â±0.05V Resistance of cell â‰¤35mâ„¦ Charging cut-off voltage of pack 4.2Â±0.05V Discharging cut-off voltage of pack 2.75Â±0.05V Rated capacity of module 6450mAh Incubator BMS Bipolar Power Supply Digatron Monitor EIS Fig. 3 Schematic diagram of experimental equipment Table 2. Test item of heating experiment Initial temperature Frequency Amplitude(A) Heating time -20â„ƒ 8 Hz 1C, 1.5C, 2C 20 min -15â„ƒ 8 Hz 1C, 1.5C, 2C 20 min -15â„ƒ 1, 4, 16 Hz 2C 20 min -10â„ƒ 8 Hz 1C, 1.5C, 2C 20 min -20â„ƒ Various 1C, 1.5C, 2C 15 min -20â„ƒ 8Hz Various 15 min -20â„ƒ Various Various 15 min 4 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 4. Results and discussion 4.1. Parameters of the model Based on the experimental data of the battery in the AC heating process, the parameters identified by the genetic algorithm are: (1) Battery specific heat capacity C is 992.87 J/(kgÂ·K); (2) Heat transfer coefficient h is 14.07 W/ (m2Â·K); (3) The relationship between 0R , ctR , SEIR and T in the equivalent circuit model is shown in Fig. 4. 4.2. Model verification with constant frequency and amplitude A heating experiment was conducted, where the sinusoidal AC frequency is set to 8Hz, the amplitude is set to 1.5C and 2C, the initial incubator temperature is set to -20â„ƒ. The sinusoidal AC heating model simulation and experiment results are shown in Fig. 5 and Fig. 6.The temperature rising simulation curve of the model well reflects the actual temperature change of the battery. The maximum temperature error between simulation and experimental results is 1.35Â°C, the average temperature error is 0.27Â°C, and it is within a reasonable range. It sufficiently suggests that the model is correct and effective. The deviation between simulation and experimental results is mainly due to the insulation condition in the experiment is not ideal, and the parameters such as the heat transfer coefficient in the simulation can be modified to better simulate the experimental conditions to reduce the deviation. 0 500 1000 1500 -25 -20 -15 -10 -5 0 T em p er a tu re ( â„ƒ ) Time (s) Simulation Experiment 0 200 400 600 800 1000 1200 -25 -20 -15 -10 -5 0 T em p er a tu re ( â„ƒ ) Time (s) Simulation Experiment Fig. 4. Variation of the resistance Fig. 5. Simulation and experiment results of 1.5C/8Hz Fig. 6. Simulation and experiment results of 2C/8Hz 4.3. Optimal sinusoidal AC heating strategy The sinusoidal AC heating experiments were conducted at different amplitudes with the same frequency and at different frequencies with the same amplitude. The heat generation effect is shown in Fig. 7 and Fig. 8. 0 200 400 600 800 1000 1200 1400 -25 -20 -15 -10 -5 0 T em pe ra tu re ï¼ˆ â„ƒ ï¼‰ Time (s) 1C 8Hz 1.5C 8Hz 2C 8Hz -200 0 200 400 600 800 1000 1200 1400 -15 -10 -5 0 5 T em p er a tu re ( â„ƒ ) Time (s) 2C 1Hz 2C 4Hz 2C 8Hz 2C 16Hz Fig. 7. Heat effect of different amplitudes Fig. 8. Heat effect of different frequencies Fig. 9. Evolution of the total impedance. It can be seen from Fig. 7, the heat generation effect becomes better with the increase of the current amplitude at the same frequency. However, when the current amplitude is too large, the voltage of the battery may exceed the safety limit. Over-voltage and under-voltage will cause side reactions, the electrolyte and the active material decomposed, a large amount of heat released, gas generated, and thermal runaway of the battery. It can be seen from Fig. 8, the heat generation effect becomes better with the current frequency decreases at the same amplitudes. However, during the experiment, it can be observed that when the frequency is low and the 566 Jun-qiu Li et al. / Energy Procedia 152 (2018) 562â€“567 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 5 amplitude is large, such as 1 Hz, 4 Hz, and 8 Hz, with an amplitude of 2C, the battery voltage significantly exceeds the safety voltage limit. Furthermore, the lower the frequency, the more exceed safety limit. This is because the total impedance of the battery increases as the frequency decreases, and the lower the temperature, the faster the total impedance increases as the frequency decreases. Firstly, simulation and experiment, respectively at -20 Â°C, -10 Â°C, 0 Â°C, are conducted within the current frequency range of 100Hz, as shown in the subgraphs in Fig. 9-12. The total impedance and real part of impedance decrease with increasing current frequency, and the lower the temperature, the larger the value, as shown in Fig. 9 and Fig. 10. Combined with the equation (7) , the maximum safe current amplitude is shown in Fig. 11. It increases with frequency at each temperature. Then, according to the equation (6) , the heat generation rate can be shown in Fig. 12. The heat generation rate increases with frequency at each temperature within 100 Hz, which is similar to the maximum safe current amplitude. However, when the frequency exceeds 100Hz, as shown in Fig. 9-12, the total impedance decreases first and then increases with increasing frequency. There is a minimum value, and the higher the temperature, the lower the frequency corresponding to the minimum value, as shown by "*" in Fig. 9. The real part of impedance decreases as the current frequency increases. The maximum safe current amplitude increases first and then decreases with increasing frequency. There is a maximum value, and the higher the temperature, the lower the frequency corresponding to the maximum value. The change of heat generation rate is similar to that of the maximum safe current amplitude. Fig. 10. Evolution of the real part of impedance Fig. 11. Evolution of the current limit Fig. 12. Evolution of the heat generation rate In order to obtain the maximum heat generation rate under the constraints of the equation (7) , equation (1) , (2) , (7) , (9) are substituted into the equation (6) . Substitute the parameters identified at each temperature into the derivative of heat generation rate 'q , solving the equation ' 0q ï€½ to obtain the optimal frequency at each temperature. Then the maximum safe current amplitude and maximum heat generation rate can be determined. The partial results obtained are shown in Table 3 (SOC = 20%). The optimal frequency gradually decreases, the maximum safe current amplitude gradually increases, and the maximum heat generation rate gradually increases as the temperature increases. Table 3. The parameters of the maximum heat generation rate(SOC=20%) Temperature(â„ƒ) Optimal Frequency(Hz) Current Limit(A) Maximum Heat Generation Rate(W) -20 4500 27.71 8.40 -10 4030 29.91 9.39 0 3550 32.05 10.31 0 200 400 600 800 1000 -20 -15 -10 -5 0 5 10 T em p er a tu re ( â„ƒ ) Time (s) SOC=40% SOC=20% Fig. 13(a) Temperature-adaptive amplitude; (b) Temperature-adaptive frequency Fig. 14. The simulated curve with optimal strategy 6 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 Therefore, a temperature-adaptive SAC heating method is proposed in this paper. Temperature-adaptive current amplitude and frequency curves are shown in Figure 13. It is easy to apply this multistep current amplitude and frequency profile to the battery by the AC heating experimental device. According to the temperature-adaptive AC heating method, the heat generation rate of the battery is always at a relatively large value and gradually increases as the temperature increases. We can clearly see from Fig. 14 that the battery can be heated from -20 Â°C to 0 Â°C within 480 s(SOC=20%), which is satisfactory. Furthermore, the smaller the SOC, the better the heat effect. 5. Conclusion A rapid and safe heating strategy for batteries with sinusoidal AC at low temperatures is proposed. It is found that in the high frequency region, the safe maximum current amplitude is larger and the heat generation rate that can be achieved is larger. Firstly, the thermo-electric coupled model of sinusoidal AC heating for lithium-ion power batteries is established. Then the parameters of the model are identified by genetic algorithm. Next, sinusoidal AC heating experiments with different current amplitudes and frequencies are conducted in different low temperature environments, which verify the correctness and validity of the model. The constraint of safety voltage limit is introduced to obtain the optimal strategy in this paper. The optimal strategy experimental bench is under construction and will be studied further. This strategy can effectively improve the low temperature performance of the battery and promote the popularization and application of electric vehicles. Acknowledgements The authors would like to thank the Collaborative Innovation Center of Electric Vehicles in Beijing Institute of Technology for the support of this research project. References [1] Haijun Ruan, Jiuchun Jiang, Bingxiang Sun, Weige Zhang, Wenzhong Gao, Le Yi Wang, Zeyu Ma. A rapid low-temperature internal heating strategy with optimal frequency based on constant polarization voltage for lithium-ion batteries. Applied Energy, 177 (2016) 771â€“782. [2] Jiuchun Jiang, Haijun Ruan, Bingxiang Sun, Weige Zhang, Wenzhong Gao, Le Yi Wang, Linjing Zhang. A reduced low-temperature electro-thermal coupled model for lithium-ion batteries. Applied Energy, 177 (2016) 804â€“816. [3] Jianbo Zhang , Hao Ge , Zhe Li , Zhanming Ding. Internal heating of lithium-ion batteries using alternating current based on the heat generation model in frequency domain. Journal of Power Sources, 273(2015)1030-1037. [4] Hao Ge, Jun Huang, Jianbo Zhang, Zhe Li. Temperature-Adaptive Alternating Current Preheating of Lithium-Ion Batteries with Lithium Deposition Prevention. Journal of The Electrochemical Society, 163 (2) A290-A299 (2016). [5] Jiangong Zhu, Zechang Sun, Xuezhe Wei, Haifeng Dai. An alternating current heating method for lithium-ion batteries from subzero temperatures. International journal of energy research Int. J. Energy Res. 2016; 40:1869â€“1883. [6] Li J, Barillas JK, Guenther C, Danzer MA. A comparative study of state of charge estimation algorithms for LiFePO4 batteries used in electric vehicles. Journal of Power Sources, 2013; 230:244â€“250. Biography Jun-qiu Li, Doctor of Engineering, associate professor, engaged in the researches of energy management and control strategy for electric vehicles. Used to take charge of a national defense fund project, win a second prize of National Technology Invention Award. Jun-qiu Li et al. / Energy Procedia 152 (2018) 562â€“567 567 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 5 amplitude is large, such as 1 Hz, 4 Hz, and 8 Hz, with an amplitude of 2C, the battery voltage significantly exceeds the safety voltage limit. Furthermore, the lower the frequency, the more exceed safety limit. This is because the total impedance of the battery increases as the frequency decreases, and the lower the temperature, the faster the total impedance increases as the frequency decreases. Firstly, simulation and experiment, respectively at -20 Â°C, -10 Â°C, 0 Â°C, are conducted within the current frequency range of 100Hz, as shown in the subgraphs in Fig. 9-12. The total impedance and real part of impedance decrease with increasing current frequency, and the lower the temperature, the larger the value, as shown in Fig. 9 and Fig. 10. Combined with the equation (7) , the maximum safe current amplitude is shown in Fig. 11. It increases with frequency at each temperature. Then, according to the equation (6) , the heat generation rate can be shown in Fig. 12. The heat generation rate increases with frequency at each temperature within 100 Hz, which is similar to the maximum safe current amplitude. However, when the frequency exceeds 100Hz, as shown in Fig. 9-12, the total impedance decreases first and then increases with increasing frequency. There is a minimum value, and the higher the temperature, the lower the frequency corresponding to the minimum value, as shown by "*" in Fig. 9. The real part of impedance decreases as the current frequency increases. The maximum safe current amplitude increases first and then decreases with increasing frequency. There is a maximum value, and the higher the temperature, the lower the frequency corresponding to the maximum value. The change of heat generation rate is similar to that of the maximum safe current amplitude. Fig. 10. Evolution of the real part of impedance Fig. 11. Evolution of the current limit Fig. 12. Evolution of the heat generation rate In order to obtain the maximum heat generation rate under the constraints of the equation (7) , equation (1) , (2) , (7) , (9) are substituted into the equation (6) . Substitute the parameters identified at each temperature into the derivative of heat generation rate 'q , solving the equation ' 0q ï€½ to obtain the optimal frequency at each temperature. Then the maximum safe current amplitude and maximum heat generation rate can be determined. The partial results obtained are shown in Table 3 (SOC = 20%). The optimal frequency gradually decreases, the maximum safe current amplitude gradually increases, and the maximum heat generation rate gradually increases as the temperature increases. Table 3. The parameters of the maximum heat generation rate(SOC=20%) Temperature(â„ƒ) Optimal Frequency(Hz) Current Limit(A) Maximum Heat Generation Rate(W) -20 4500 27.71 8.40 -10 4030 29.91 9.39 0 3550 32.05 10.31 0 200 400 600 800 1000 -20 -15 -10 -5 0 5 10 T em p er a tu re ( â„ƒ ) Time (s) SOC=40% SOC=20% Fig. 13(a) Temperature-adaptive amplitude; (b) Temperature-adaptive frequency Fig. 14. The simulated curve with optimal strategy 6 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 Therefore, a temperature-adaptive SAC heating method is proposed in this paper. Temperature-adaptive current amplitude and frequency curves are shown in Figure 13. It is easy to apply this multistep current amplitude and frequency profile to the battery by the AC heating experimental device. According to the temperature-adaptive AC heating method, the heat generation rate of the battery is always at a relatively large value and gradually increases as the temperature increases. We can clearly see from Fig. 14 that the battery can be heated from -20 Â°C to 0 Â°C within 480 s(SOC=20%), which is satisfactory. Furthermore, the smaller the SOC, the better the heat effect. 5. Conclusion A rapid and safe heating strategy for batteries with sinusoidal AC at low temperatures is proposed. It is found that in the high frequency region, the safe maximum current amplitude is larger and the heat generation rate that can be achieved is larger. Firstly, the thermo-electric coupled model of sinusoidal AC heating for lithium-ion power batteries is established. Then the parameters of the model are identified by genetic algorithm. Next, sinusoidal AC heating experiments with different current amplitudes and frequencies are conducted in different low temperature environments, which verify the correctness and validity of the model. The constraint of safety voltage limit is introduced to obtain the optimal strategy in this paper. The optimal strategy experimental bench is under construction and will be studied further. This strategy can effectively improve the low temperature performance of the battery and promote the popularization and application of electric vehicles. Acknowledgements The authors would like to thank the Collaborative Innovation Center of Electric Vehicles in Beijing Institute of Technology for the support of this research project. References [1] Haijun Ruan, Jiuchun Jiang, Bingxiang Sun, Weige Zhang, Wenzhong Gao, Le Yi Wang, Zeyu Ma. A rapid low-temperature internal heating strategy with optimal frequency based on constant polarization voltage for lithium-ion batteries. Applied Energy, 177 (2016) 771â€“782. [2] Jiuchun Jiang, Haijun Ruan, Bingxiang Sun, Weige Zhang, Wenzhong Gao, Le Yi Wang, Linjing Zhang. A reduced low-temperature electro-thermal coupled model for lithium-ion batteries. Applied Energy, 177 (2016) 804â€“816. [3] Jianbo Zhang , Hao Ge , Zhe Li , Zhanming Ding. Internal heating of lithium-ion batteries using alternating current based on the heat generation model in frequency domain. Journal of Power Sources, 273(2015)1030-1037. [4] Hao Ge, Jun Huang, Jianbo Zhang, Zhe Li. Temperature-Adaptive Alternating Current Preheating of Lithium-Ion Batteries with Lithium Deposition Prevention. Journal of The Electrochemical Society, 163 (2) A290-A299 (2016). [5] Jiangong Zhu, Zechang Sun, Xuezhe Wei, Haifeng Dai. An alternating current heating method for lithium-ion batteries from subzero temperatures. International journal of energy research Int. J. Energy Res. 2016; 40:1869â€“1883. [6] Li J, Barillas JK, Guenther C, Danzer MA. A comparative study of state of charge estimation algorithms for LiFePO4 batteries used in electric vehicles. Journal of Power Sources, 2013; 230:244â€“250. Biography Jun-qiu Li, Doctor of Engineering, associate professor, engaged in the researches of energy management and control strategy for electric vehicles. Used to take charge of a national defense fund project, win a second prize of National Technology Invention Award. ters of the maximum heat generation rate(SOC=20%) Temperature(â„ƒ) Optimal Frequency(Hz) Current Limit(A) Maximum Heat Generation Rate(W) -20 4500 27.71 8.40 -10 4030 29.91 9.39 0 3550 32.05 10.31 0 200 400 600 800 1000 -20 -15 -10 -5 0 5 10 T em p er a tu re ( â„ƒ ) Time (s) SOC=40% SOC=20% Fig. 13(a) Temperature-adaptive amplitude; (b) Temperature-adaptive frequency Fig. 14. The simulated curve with optimal strategy 6 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 Therefore, a temperature-adaptive SAC heating method is proposed in this paper. Temperature-adaptive current amplitude and frequency curves are shown in Figure 13. It is easy to apply this multistep current amplitude and frequency profile to the battery by the AC heating experimental device. According to the temperature-adaptive AC heating method, the heat generation rate of the battery is always at a relatively large value and gradually increases as the temperature increases. We can clearly see from Fig. 14 that the battery can be heated from -20 Â°C to 0 Â°C within 480 s(SOC=20%), which is satisfactory. Furthermore, the smaller the SOC, the better the heat effect. 5. Conclusion A rapid and safe heating strategy for batteries with sinusoidal AC at low temperatures is proposed. It is found that in the high frequency region, the safe maximum current amplitude is larger and the heat generation rate that can be achieved is larger. Firstly, the thermo-electric coupled model of sinusoidal AC heating for lithium-ion power batteries is established. Then the parameters of the model are identified by genetic algorithm. Next, sinusoidal AC heating experiments with different current amplitudes and frequencies are conducted in different low temperature environments, which verify the correctness and validity of the model. The constraint of safety voltage limit is introduced to obtain the optimal strategy in this paper. The optimal strategy experimental bench is under construction and will be studied further. This strategy can effectively improve the low temperature performance of the battery and promote the popularization and application of electric vehicles. Acknowledgements The authors would like to thank the Collaborative Innovation Center of Electric Vehicles in Bei

head.ce:title: Lithium-ion Batteries Modeling and Optimization Strategies for Sinusoidal Alternating Current Heating at Low Temperature

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center of Electric Vehicles in Beijing, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing, 100081,China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Electric Vehicles in Beijing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A thermo-electric coupled model of sinusoidal alternating current (AC) heating for lithium-ion power batteries is proposed in this paper. The model combines the equivalent circuit model with the heat generation model in frequency domain. The model is validated against the experimental results of sinusoidal AC heating with different amplitudes and frequencies of current. An optimal strategy of sinusoidal AC heating based on battery safe operating voltage limit is proposed . It is found that in the high frequency region, the maximum current amplitude under the safety voltage limit is larger, the maximum heat generation rate of the battery is larger, and the battery can be heated from -20°C to 0°C within 480s.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A rapid low-temperature internal heating strategy with optimal frequency based on constant polarization voltage for lithium-ion batteries
A reduced low-temperature electro-thermal coupled model for lithium-ion batteries
Internal heating of lithium-ion batteries using alternating current based on the heat generation model in frequency domain
Temperature-Adaptive Alternating Current Preheating of Lithium-Ion Batteries with Lithium Deposition Prevention
An alternating current heating method for lithium-ion batteries from subzero temperatures
International journal of energy research Int. J. Energy Res.
A comparative study of state of charge estimation algorithms for LiFePO4 batteries used in electric vehicles
